exploration_log.aha_moment: The `#[Depends]` attribute is only processed in the `forMethod()` switch statement, and the `$reflector` there is a `ReflectionMethod` which provides filename and line for the InvalidAttributeException constructor.
exploration_log.hindsight: Also applied the same check to DependsUsingDeepClone and DependsUsingShallowClone for completeness, which the task implied but didn't explicitly mention.

key_decisions.reasoning: All three variants accept a method name as their argument and would have the same misuse pattern; consistency requires all three to validate

solve_notes: Added `str_contains($attributeInstance->methodName(), '::')` checks in the `Depends`, `DependsUsingDeepClone`, and `DependsUsingShallowClone` cases in `forMethod()`. When the method name contains `::`, an `InvalidAttributeException` is thrown with a message directing the developer to use the appropriate `#[DependsExternal*]` variant.
task_text:
Fix AttributeParser silently mishandling #[Depends] with a class-qualified method name

`AttributeParser` parses `#[Depends]` attributes by passing the entire argument string directly to `Metadata::dependsOnMethod()` as the method name on the current class. When a developer writes `#[Depends('OtherTest::testSetup')]` intending to declare a cross-class dependency, the full string `'OtherTest::testSetup'` is used as the method name without any splitting or validation. The dependency is registered against a method whose name contains `::`, which never matches any real method, so the declared dependency is silently ignored. Fix `AttributeParser.php` so that when a `#[Depends]` value contains `::`, it detects this case and throws an `InvalidAttributeException` with a message directing the developer to use `#[DependsExternal]` (or `#[DependsExternalUsingDeepClone]` / `#[DependsExternalUsingShallowClone]`) for cross-class dependencies.

tier_difference_reasoning: minimum_sufficient is the forMethod() method being edited. thrash_preventing adds InvalidAttributeException because an agent needs to see its constructor signature to call it correctly — without it, the agent would need to search for it mid-implementation.
